perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information does not include information, technical data or know-how which: (i) is in the possession of the receiving party at the time of disclosure as shown by the receiving party's files and records immediately prior to the time of disclosure; (ii) prior or after the time of disclosure becomes part of the public knowledge or literature, not as a result of any inaction or action of the receiving party; or (iii) is appr oved for release by the Discloser in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information does not include information, technical data or know-how which: (i) is in the possession of the receiving party at the time of disclosure as shown by the receiving party's oral statements immediately prior to the time of disclosure; (ii) prior or after the time of disclosure becomes part of the public knowledge or literature, as a result of any inaction or action of the receiving party; or (iii) is not appr oved for release by the Discloser in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' is altered. Specifically, (i) now relies on 'oral statements' instead of 'files and records' for proof of prior possession and (ii) includes information that becomes public knowledge as a result of any action of the receiving party. Also, clause (iii) is negated, creating contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Law - Inevitable Disclosure Doctrine and Misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: The change contradicts trade secret laws. By allowing 'oral statements' as proof and including information becoming public due to the receiving party's actions, the contract weakens protection against misappropriation and inevitable disclosure of trade secrets as defined under federal law, specifically regarding reasonable measures to protect such information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a contradiction by weakening the protection of confidential information. The original text requires 'files and records' as proof of prior possession, offering a verifiable standard. The changed text allows 'oral statements,' which are less reliable and verifiable, thus weakening the protection of trade secrets. Additionally, the changed text includes information becoming public due to the receiving party’s actions, contradicting the reasonable measures expected to protect confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Economic Espionage Act, indicating federal interest in protecting trade secrets, and the changes made to the contract weaken these protections.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that export controlled information shall not be disclosed to any unauthorized foreign person, firm, country, incl uding foreign persons employed by Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that export controlled information shall not be disclosed to any unauthorized foreign person, firm, country, excluding foreign persons employed by Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: The word 'including' has been changed to 'excluding'. This fundamentally alters the meaning of the sentence. Now, it suggests that disclosure *is* permitted to foreign persons employed by the Discloser, which is a direct contradiction to export control regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the EAR, which restricts the release of controlled technology to foreign nationals, even if employed by a U.S. company. The EAR regulates the export and re-export of sensitive technologies to prevent them from falling into the wrong hands. By permitting disclosure to foreign persons employed by the Discloser, the modified text creates a loophole that violates export control laws.

perturbation.justification: The modification, changing 'including' to 'excluding', directly contradicts export control regulations. The original text prohibited disclosure to unauthorized foreign persons, including those employed by the Discloser. The modified text now permits disclosure to foreign persons employed by the Discloser. This creates a loophole that violates export control laws, as indicated by the Law Explanation, because it potentially allows sensitive information to reach unauthorized foreign nationals, even if they are employed by the Discloser. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 15 CFR 734.2, which falls under EAR regulations and deals with export control. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Document not Found' error, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both explain why the change creates a legal contradiction to export control laws.

perturbation.original_text: Specifically, Recipient agrees to comply with U.S. export control laws and regulations on all information it receives that is labeled “Export Controlled” and on information it creates in the scope of work on this project that is export c ontrolled.
perturbation.changed_text: Specifically, Recipient agrees to try to comply with U.S. export control laws and regulations on all information it receives that is labeled “Export Controlled” and on information it creates in the scope of work on this project that is export c ontrolled.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to comply' to 'agrees to try to comply' weakens the contractual obligation, creating ambiguity and potentially undermining the enforcement of export control regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification conflicts with the AECA, which mandates strict adherence to export control laws for defense articles and services. The 'try to comply' language introduces an element of discretion that undermines the mandatory nature of the AECA, potentially leading to non-compliance and violations of national security regulations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Home - DDTC Public Portal - DDTC Public Portal Loading...
perturbation.justification: The original text states the recipient 'agrees to comply,' establishing a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'agrees to try to comply,' introducing ambiguity about the level of commitment required. This modification undermines the strict adherence to export control laws mandated by the AECA, according to the Law Explanation. While the provided law snippets are unusable, the Law Explanation indicates a conflict with regulations requiring strict adherence, and the change directly weakens the contractual obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient further warrants that all individuals that will be given access to this info rmation are U.S. citizens or permanent U.S. residents with a valid permanent resident cards, and that the information disclosed to Recipient will be stored securely.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient further warrants that all individuals that will be given access to this info rmation are U.S. citizens or temporary U.S. residents with a valid temporary resident cards, and that the information disclosed to Recipient will be stored on the cloud.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'permanent U.S. residents' to 'temporary U.S. residents' and 'stored securely' to 'stored on the cloud' introduces two contradictions. Disclosing export-controlled information to temporary residents may violate export control regulations. Cloud storage may not meet security requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Guidelines
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts NIST guidelines, which outline security controls for protecting controlled unclassified information (CUI). Storing export-controlled information on a generic cloud platform may not meet the stringent security requirements outlined by NIST, potentially violating federal regulations regarding the protection of sensitive data. Also, only US Persons (citizen or permanent resident) can have access to export controlled information.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'permanent U.S. residents' to 'temporary U.S. residents' is a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states, 'only US Persons (citizen or permanent resident) can have access to export controlled information.' The modified contract language potentially violates federal regulations by allowing temporary residents to access export-controlled information, which contradicts the requirement that access be limited to U.S. citizens or permanent residents.

perturbation.original_text: The state cour ts within Monroe County New York and/or the federal courts located in Western New York shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The state cour ts within Monroe County New York or any court the Recipient chooses shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the recipient to choose any court, removing the exclusivity of the original jurisdiction and potentially creating forum shopping opportunities. This is a significant alteration to the dispute resolution process.
perturbation.law_explanation: This conflicts with general venue statutes under 28 U.S.C. § 1391 which, while allowing parties to agree on venue, are typically interpreted to require a reasonable connection to the parties or the dispute. Giving the Recipient unfettered discretion to choose any court could lead to a venue with no connection to the agreement, which may be challenged as unreasonable and unenforceable. Many courts require that a forum selection clause be 'reasonable' and not obtained through fraud or overreaching. This change opens the door to arguments that the clause is unreasonable.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The change from specifying courts in Monroe County New York and/or the federal courts located in Western New York to allowing the Recipient to choose any court contradicts the principles of venue as outlined in 28 U.S.C. § 1391. The original text established a specific, agreed-upon venue, while the modified text permits the recipient to select any court, potentially one with no connection to the agreement or the parties. This undermines the intent of venue statutes, which typically require a reasonable connection to the dispute. While 28 U.S.C. § 1391 allows parties to agree on venue, the broad discretion granted to the Recipient could lead to an unreasonable and unenforceable venue selection, as the Law Explanation suggests. Scraped Law Snippet 2, which contains 28 U.S. Code § 1391, supports this by outlining the general rules for venue, which emphasize connections to the defendant's residence or where the events giving rise to the claim occurred, principles that are undermined by the change.
